expectations.text: States that Premium Bond prizes are tax-free
expectations.evidence: Response clearly states: 'Premium Bond prizes are **completely tax-free** and do **not** need to be declared on a Self Assessment return.'
Response states: 'Your total taxable savings interest is **£1,600**' and provides breakdown: 'Marcus account interest: £870, Nationwide account interest: £320, NS&I Income Bonds interest: £410'

expectations.text: Applies the PSA correctly (£1,000 for basic rate taxpayer at £48,000)
expectations.evidence: Response states: 'As a basic rate taxpayer, you are entitled to a **Personal Savings Allowance of £1,000** for 2024/25' and applies it: 'Your total taxable savings interest is **£1,600**... Personal Savings Allowance: -£1,000, **Interest above PSA: £600**'

expectations.text: Calculates tax due on £600 excess interest above PSA
expectations.evidence: Response calculates: 'The £600 above your PSA is taxable at the basic rate of **20%**, giving a tax liability on savings interest of **£120**.'

expectations.text: Explains that banks pay interest gross since 2016 (no automatic tax deduction)
expectations.evidence: Response explains: 'Since then, banks and building societies pay all interest **gross** — no tax is deducted at source.'

expectations.text: Explains how HMRC collects the tax (tax code adjustment or Self Assessment)
expectations.evidence: Response explains both options: '**Option 1 — HMRC adjusts your PAYE tax code**: HMRC receives interest data directly from your banks and NS&I. For relatively modest amounts like £600 of taxable savings interest, HMRC will often collect the tax by reducing your PAYE tax code... **Option 2 — Self Assessment is required**: You would need to file a Self Assessment return if...'
